content:
target shape: 3 services, not 4. merge orders+billing into one "commerce core" — strong consistency on order↔payment requires single local tx, splitting them forces sagas you banned. fulfillment and inventory split out.

services:
- commerce-core: order lifecycle + payment intent/auth/capture. owns orders, payments, refunds. single Postgres. local ACID across order state + payment state.
- inventory: stock levels, reservations, replenishment. own Postgres.
- fulfillment: pick, pack, ship, tracking. own Postgres.

protocols:
- sync (gRPC internal): inventory.reserve during checkout — need answer now. payment auth to PSP already sync, stays sync. all other cross-service calls async.
- async (Kafka): commerce-core emits order.created, order.paid, order.cancelled, refund.issued. fulfillment consumes order.paid. inventory emits stock.adjusted, reservation.expired.
- outbox pattern in every producer. inbox + idempotency keys in every consumer. no 2PC anywhere.

data ownership rules:
- one writer per table. no shared DB. no cross-service joins.
- reference data (customer, product) owned by source-of-truth service, replicated via event stream into local read models. stale reads acceptable except at checkout.
- inventory stock: only inventory writes. commerce-core calls reserve(orderId, ttl=15min). reservation auto-expires on timeout → inventory emits reservation.released → commerce-core cancels order if still pending.
- payment state: only commerce-core writes. PSP webhook hits commerce-core, idempotent on providerTxnId.

order-to-payment consistency (core constraint):
- single Postgres tx in commerce-core covers: order row transition + payment row transition + outbox row insert.
- no distributed tx because payment capture result + order status live in same DB.
- external PSP is eventually consistent; reconciler job sweeps stuck intents hourly.

reservation flow (replaces distributed tx):
1. checkout → commerce-core calls inventory.reserve (sync gRPC, returns reservationId).
2. commerce-core calls PSP auth (sync).
3. local tx: order=CONFIRMED, payment=AUTHORIZED, outbox[order.confirmed].
4. async: inventory consumes order.confirmed → converts reservation to commitment. if inventory fails → compensating order.cancel event → commerce-core refunds + cancels.
5. TTL on reservation bounds the compensation window.

migration 6mo / 8 eng:
- team split: 3 on commerce-core, 3 on inventory+fulfillment, 2 platform (Kafka schemas, deploy pipeline, outbox lib, observability).
- strangler via API gateway in front of monolith from week 1.
- weeks 1-2: platform foundations — outbox table in monolith, Kafka topics + schema registry, gRPC scaffolding, per-service CI templates.
- weeks 3-6: extract inventory first. smallest blast radius, clear API. dual-write from monolith via outbox, cutover readers, drop old tables.
- weeks 7-12: extract fulfillment. pure downstream consumer of order.paid, low risk, builds team Kafka muscle.
- weeks 13-22: carve commerce-core out of monolith. keep orders+billing together. schema migration to own DB using logical replication, shadow-read validation, cutover writes behind feature flag.
- weeks 23-26: decommission monolith, split test suites (target <3min each), enable independent deploys, write runbooks.

wins on original pain:
- release coupling: 3 pipelines, 3 deploy cadences.
- merge conflicts: 3 repos, ownership boundaries enforced by code review.
- 12min test suite: becomes ~3min × 3 parallel.
- blocking migrations: each service migrates its own DB, no cross-team coordination.

risks to watch:
- commerce-core stays fat — accept it. splitting order from payment is the trap.
- Kafka schema drift — mandate schema registry + contract tests from week 1.
- reservation TTL tuning — start 15min, instrument expiry rate, tune from data.
- dual-write window during each extraction — keep short, automate reconciliation checks.

2yr runway is fine for 6mo migration — leaves 18mo to exploit the new shape before next re-org.

three services from prior turn: commerce-core, inventory, fulfillment.

commerce-core owns exclusively:
- orders (id, customer_id, status, currency, total, created_at, confirmed_at, cancelled_at)
- order_items (id, order_id, sku, qty, unit_price, reservation_id — value ref into inventory, not FK)
- payments (id, order_id, psp_ref, status, amount, authorized_at, captured_at)
- refunds (id, payment_id, amount, reason, status)
- customers (id, email, name, default_shipping_address, created_at)
- customer_addresses (id, customer_id, line1/line2/city/...)
- outbox_commerce

commerce-core reads from others:
- inventory.reserve(sku, qty, orderId, ttl) — sync gRPC at checkout, returns reservationId.
- inventory events reservation.committed / reservation.released — async Kafka, updates local order_items.reservation_status read field.
- fulfillment events shipment.dispatched / shipment.delivered — async Kafka, updates orders.fulfillment_status denormalized column (read model only, not source of truth).
- PSP (external) auth/capture — sync HTTPS, not an internal service.

commerce-core publishes:
- order.created (on checkout start)
- order.confirmed (payment authorized + reservation held — carries full order payload incl shipping address, for event-carried state transfer to fulfillment)
- order.paid (payment captured)
- order.cancelled
- payment.authorized, payment.captured, payment.failed
- refund.issued
- customer.created, customer.updated

inventory owns exclusively:
- stock_items (sku, warehouse_id, on_hand, available_to_promise)
- inventory_holds (id, sku, warehouse_id, qty, order_id_ref, status, expires_at) — note: holds live here, not in commerce-core, because inventory is authority on stock and single-writer rule forbids dual ownership.
- stock_movements (append-only ledger for audit)
- replenishments (id, sku, qty, eta, po_ref)
- outbox_inventory

inventory reads from others:
- order.confirmed event — async consume, converts matching hold into committed deduction.
- order.cancelled event — async consume, releases matching hold early.
- no sync calls outbound. no reads of other services' DBs.

inventory publishes:
- reservation.created (ack of sync reserve call, also emitted async for auditors)
- reservation.committed
- reservation.released (TTL expiry OR explicit cancellation)
- stock.adjusted (any on_hand change, with reason code)
- stock.low_watermark (threshold crossed)

fulfillment owns exclusively:
- shipments (id, order_id_ref, carrier, tracking_number, status, shipped_at, delivered_at)
- shipment_items (id, shipment_id, sku, qty)
- pick_lists (id, warehouse_id, status, assigned_picker)
- pick_list_items
- tracking_events (append-only carrier webhooks)
- outbox_fulfillment

fulfillment reads from others:
- order.paid event — async consume, payload carries orderId + items + shipping address + customer name. no callback needed.
- inventory.locate(sku, warehouse_id) — sync gRPC during pick planning, lightweight lookup. alternative: local read model fed by stock.adjusted events if latency matters.
- no reads of commerce-core DB. no reads of inventory DB.

fulfillment publishes:
- shipment.created
- shipment.dispatched (with tracking_number)
- shipment.delivered
- shipment.failed (carrier reject, address invalid, etc.)

ownership invariants enforced:
- each table has exactly one owner listed above.
- order_id_ref and customer_id_ref in inventory_holds / shipments are value-only pointers — no cross-DB FKs, no cross-schema queries.
- customers lives in commerce-core because no separate identity service exists at team size 8. emit customer.* events so extraction stays cheap if SSO/multi-app arrives later.
- product/SKU master not in original 4 domains — assume commerce-core owns products table for now, treat SKU as shared vocabulary. carve out catalog service only if it grows teeth.
- order_items.reservation_id stores inventory's reservationId as opaque string, not relational link.
- shipping address copied into order.paid event payload (event-carried state) so fulfillment never calls back for it — decouples and survives customer address edits after order.

forbidden patterns:
- no cross-service DB joins.
- no shared read-replicas mounted by multiple services.
- no service-A writes to service-B table ever, including during migration (use outbox from both sides instead).
- no synchronous chain longer than commerce-core → inventory.reserve + commerce-core → PSP. everything else async.

orchestrated saga, not choreographed. orchestrator lives in commerce-core — one brain, easier to reason about payment. saga state persisted in same Postgres as orders, so every transition is one local tx: update saga row + insert outbox row. no 2PC because all durable state sits in one DB; external calls happen after commit, retried via idempotency keys.

states (order.status column):
- DRAFT — cart built, no side effects yet
- RESERVING — sync inventory.reserve in flight
- RESERVED — hold obtained, reservation_id stored
- AUTHORIZING — PSP auth call in flight
- AUTHORIZED — auth succeeded, not yet captured
- CAPTURING — capture call in flight
- PAID — captured + reservation committed
- FULFILLING — shipment(s) in progress
- FULFILLED — all shipments delivered (terminal)
- CANCELLING — compensation running
- CANCELLED — terminal after cancel-before-capture
- REFUNDING — compensation after capture
- REFUNDED — terminal after cancel-after-capture
- PARTIAL_FULFILLED — some items shipped, rest unrecoverable
- MANUAL_REVIEW — ops must resolve
- FAILED — terminal, no money moved

happy path:
DRAFT → RESERVING → RESERVED → AUTHORIZING → AUTHORIZED → CAPTURING → PAID → FULFILLING → FULFILLED

compensations (forward-undo, never backward rewind):
- RESERVING fails → FAILED (hold never existed, nothing to release)
- RESERVED + auth declined → CANCELLING → release_reservation → CANCELLED
- AUTHORIZED + capture declined → CANCELLING → void_auth + release_reservation → CANCELLED
- PAID + inventory short → REFUNDING → psp_refund + stock_adjust → REFUNDED
- FULFILLING + partial ship → PARTIAL_FULFILLED → refund_delta → REFUNDED_PARTIAL
- any unknown error past 3 retries → MANUAL_REVIEW, alert ops

transition invariant:
every write is CAS against expected prior state.
UPDATE orders SET status='AUTHORIZING', version=version+1 WHERE id=? AND status='RESERVED' AND version=?.
rows_affected=1 or abort. no distributed lock needed; Postgres row-level lock inside local tx is enough.

idempotency (every step):
- saga_step_id = hash(order_id, step_name, attempt_n).
- outbound HTTP/gRPC carries Idempotency-Key header = saga_step_id.
- inventory.reserve idempotent on reservation_id = order_id (one hold per order, re-reserve returns existing).
- PSP auth idempotent on order_id+attempt; retries reuse same key, PSP returns prior outcome.
- every Kafka consumer has inbox table (source_topic, event_id) unique — insert-ignore dedups.
- saga_step_log append-only, records input_hash + output_hash per attempt for audit.

timeouts (deadline_at per state):
- RESERVING: 5s — assume lost reply, mark FAILED, inventory's own TTL cleans hold.
- RESERVED: 15min — matches hold TTL; auto-cancel if user never submits payment.
- AUTHORIZING: 30s — timer triggers PSP lookup by idempotency key, three outcomes: success→AUTHORIZED, decline→CANCELLING, unknown→retry bounded.
- AUTHORIZED: 5min — capture or void; never leave auth dangling, merchant fees + hold on customer card.
- CAPTURING: 30s — same lookup-by-idemkey dance.
- FULFILLING: per-SLA, days; escalation event not saga compensation.
- sweeper job SELECT ... FROM saga_instance WHERE deadline_at<now AND state IN (non_terminal) LIMIT N FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED. multiple workers safe, SKIP LOCKED avoids contention, no distributed lock.

critical scenario 1 — payment timeout (AUTHORIZING, PSP reply lost):
- local tx already committed "auth requested" with idempotency_key K before calling PSP. so state survives crash.
- on 30s timeout, reconciler calls PSP.lookup(K).
- outcome success → CAS to AUTHORIZED, schedule capture.
- outcome declined → CAS to CANCELLING, emit reservation.release, then CANCELLED.
- outcome unknown → retry up to 3, then MANUAL_REVIEW with all evidence logged.
- double-charge impossible because same K on every retry; PSP returns first outcome.

critical scenario 2 — inventory released after payment:
- race: PAID reached but reservation.released arrives (TTL expired during slow capture, or inventory bug).
- mitigation first: right before capture, commerce-core calls inventory.extend_reservation(reservation_id, +10min) — atomic extend-or-fail. if fail, abort to CANCELLING before charging.
- if race still happens post-PAID: consume reservation.released for a PAID order → transition PAID → INVENTORY_SHORT (sub-state).
- resolution ladder:
  a. attempt re-reserve (inventory.reserve again) — if stock back, convert to committed, resume FULFILLING.
  b. if partial stock available, plan partial ship, refund delta.
  c. if zero stock, REFUNDING → full refund → REFUNDED + customer notification event.
- customer never charged without recourse; money-moved states always have a refund path.

critical scenario 3 — partial fulfillment rollback:
- fulfillment tried to pick 3 items, shipped 2, third damaged at warehouse.
- fulfillment emits shipment.partial{order_id, shipped_items[], missing_items[], reason}.
- commerce-core consumes: CAS FULFILLING → PARTIAL_FULFILLED.
- policy flag on order (set at checkout) decides:
  - auto_refund_missing: compute delta = sum(missing_items.price), call PSP.refund(delta) with idem_key=order_id+"partial_refund", emit refund.issued, inventory absorbs write-off via its own stock_movement. terminal REFUNDED_PARTIAL.
  - manual_review: state WAITING_REVIEW, ops tool has buttons for refund / reship / credit.
- no backward rewind of shipped items — forward-undo only, because physical world doesn't rewind.
- inventory separately reconciles via shipment.shipped and shipment.partial events; adjusts on_hand. single writer preserved.

no 2PC proof sketch:
- every state change = one local Postgres tx touching orders + saga_instance + outbox_commerce only.
- outbox publisher emits to Kafka at-least-once. consumers dedup via inbox.
- external side effects (PSP, inventory.reserve) happen after commit. if process dies between commit and external call, timer rediscovers state and retries with same idempotency key.
- compensations are themselves sagas-of-one-step with their own local tx + outbox + timeout, composable.

no distributed lock proof sketch:
- concurrency across workers: CAS on status+version column; exactly one worker advances state.
- concurrency within a worker: SELECT ... FOR UPDATE on orders row inside local tx — Postgres native, not distributed.
- concurrency across services: none — inventory and fulfillment never mutate commerce-core state; they emit events, commerce-core CAS-applies.

retry budgets:
reserve=3, authorize=3, capture=5, refund=∞-with-backoff (refund must eventually succeed or page ops), release_reservation=5. after budget exhausted → MANUAL_REVIEW, never silent data loss.

observability must-haves:
- saga_step_log as source of truth for "what happened to order X".
- metric: orders stuck in non-terminal state > p99 deadline.
- metric: compensation rate per state — rising auth→cancel rate = PSP issue, rising PAID→REFUND rate = inventory issue.
- alert on MANUAL_REVIEW queue depth.

eventual-consistency is acceptable inside the system; it is dangerous when user-visible expectations get violated. below: every window where our design leaks, its cause, mitigation, bound.

window 1 — "I just placed an order, where is it?"
cause: commerce-core write is strongly consistent, but order history is usually served from CQRS read model fed by Kafka projection. between commit and projection apply, GET /orders returns stale list.
risk: user refreshes, panics, places order twice.
mitigation: read-your-writes. route /orders reads for user U for 60s after last write from U directly to primary (or to session-sticky read replica that streams synchronously). alternatively, write path returns full order object and client optimistically prepends to local cache.
bound: order visible in /orders within 500ms of 2xx on POST /orders. hard SLO.

window 2 — PDP availability stale (oversell illusion):
cause: inventory.reserve is sync, but stock.adjusted → product-listing ATP read model is async.
risk: shopper B sees "in stock" for a SKU shopper A just reserved. B adds to cart, hits checkout, reserve fails, bad UX at the highest-intent moment.
mitigation: two-layer display. PDP shows coarse bucket ("in stock / limited / out") from eventually-consistent index. add-to-cart does NOT reserve; cart→checkout transition performs authoritative reserve and handles failure with clear message + suggested alternatives. for top-K hot SKUs, bypass projection and query inventory directly with 50ms cache.
bound: PDP ATP refresh within 2s of stock change. add-to-cart→reserve round-trip <300ms p95. hot-SKU direct read <100ms.

window 3 — address edited after submit:
cause: order.paid event payload carries shipping address (event-carried state). fulfillment will ship to that snapshot. if user edits profile address post-submit expecting the in-flight order to follow, it won't.
risk: parcel goes to old address. expensive failure.
mitigation: this is not a consistency window, it's a UX contract. freeze the address affordance once order.status >= CONFIRMED. show "shipping to <address> — contact support to change before dispatch" banner on order detail page. within-dispatch-window edits allowed via a change_shipping_address saga that coordinates fulfillment.
bound: zero window by UX. edit lockout on CONFIRMED; support can mutate within 30min if shipment not yet created.

window 4 — "payment confirmed" UI shown before commit-to-fulfill:
cause: user sees AUTHORIZED state as "payment confirmed," but between AUTHORIZED and PAID the reservation could expire or commit could fail, leading to refund.
risk: user screenshots success, later gets cancellation email.
mitigation: do not surface success until state=PAID (captured + reservation committed). UI during AUTHORIZING/AUTHORIZED shows "finalizing your order" spinner with max-expected time. extend_reservation called before capture to shrink race. if PAID not reached in 10s, UI flips to "still processing — we'll email you within 2 minutes."
bound: PAID within 5s p95, 10s p99. if exceeded, switch to deferred notification UX.

window 5 — shipment status not reflected on order page:
cause: fulfillment emits shipment.dispatched → commerce-core consumer updates orders.fulfillment_status read field → client polls. three hops.
risk: customer gets carrier tracking email before order page shows "shipped," confusion and support ticket.
mitigation: push update via server-sent events or websocket subscribed to order_id. fallback poll every 15s on active order page. include tracking_number in shipment.dispatched payload so it propagates in one event, not two.
bound: order page status flip within 5s p95 of dispatched event. carrier email delay ≥30s so order page wins the race.

window 6 — partial fulfillment limbo:
cause: PARTIAL_FULFILLED state may sit for hours if manual_review policy; customer sees 2/3 shipped and no explanation.
risk: silence breeds tickets and chargebacks.
mitigation: emit customer-facing notification within minutes of transition ("one item delayed, we're resolving — no action needed, no charge for undelivered"). status page shows per-item state, not order-level only. auto-refund policy as default for low-value items; manual review only above $N threshold.
bound: customer notified within 5min of PARTIAL_FULFILLED detection. resolution (refund or reship) within 24h, tracked as ops SLA.

window 7 — cross-device order visibility:
cause: read-your-writes keyed by session cookie won't help if user hops from mobile to desktop.
risk: user places order on phone, opens laptop, doesn't see it, re-orders.
mitigation: key read-your-writes on user_id, not session. for 60s post-write, all sessions of that user_id route /orders reads to primary. alternatively, shrink projection lag target to <200ms p99 and skip the stickiness hack.
bound: cross-device visibility within 500ms of confirmation. same SLO as window 1, just stricter enforcement.

window 8 — cancel → stock back on PDP:
cause: order.cancelled → reservation.released → stock.adjusted → PDP ATP refresh. multi-hop.
risk: cancelling user expects to re-add item to cart; other shoppers want item back in listings. low-severity usually.
mitigation: cancel response returns updated availability hint for cancelling user's own cart. background reconciliation for public PDP. no special UX for other shoppers — 2s is fine.
bound: cancelling user's PDP refresh <500ms (served from inventory direct). public PDP <3s p95.

window 9 — refund visible on customer card:
cause: PSP settlement is 3-10 business days. not our system, cannot shrink.
risk: user panics at missing refund.
mitigation: explicit copy at refund time: "refunded to **** 4242, typically 3-5 business days depending on your bank." order page shows refund.issued timestamp immediately; separate line for "settled on card" remains blank or "pending" until webhook confirms.
bound: refund.issued visible in our UI <500ms (same-DB write). bank settlement uncontrolled; communicate 3-10 days.

window 10 — admin order search / analytics:
cause: CDC → warehouse/search index, minutes of lag.
risk: support agent searches, order "missing," ends up re-issuing.
mitigation: admin tool has a fallback "search by exact order_id" that hits primary. bulk search uses index with visible "data freshness: 2min ago" timestamp.
bound: indexed search freshness <5min; direct lookup always real-time.

prioritization by blast radius:
- windows 1, 2, 4 affect conversion and trust. sub-second bounds, engineered carefully.
- windows 3, 6 are UX contract issues, solved by copy and affordance more than infra.
- windows 5, 7, 8 are polish; 2-5s bounds acceptable.
- windows 9, 10 are expectation-setting, not latency problems.

global default: if a window can't meet bound, the correct fix is usually to (a) read from authoritative source on that path, (b) change UX to stop promising synchrony we don't deliver, or (c) include the needed data in the event payload to collapse a hop. adding more caching rarely helps; it deepens the staleness.

we are splitting our orders platform from one large Django application into three focused services — a commerce core that handles orders and payments together, an inventory service, and a fulfillment service — each with its own database, its own deploy pipeline, and a clear owning team. today all eight engineers ship through one release train, tests take twelve minutes, database migrations block deploys, and teams collide on merges. the split fixes those bottlenecks and lets the org scale headcount without scaling pain. critically, we are keeping orders and payments inside the same service so that charging a customer and confirming their order remain one atomic operation — this avoids the most common and most expensive microservices mistake (splitting payment from order, then inventing fragile workarounds). every other interaction between services uses durable events rather than distributed transactions, which is standard industry practice at our scale and stack (Kafka + Postgres + k8s).

timeline: six months, phased via strangler pattern — extract inventory first (lowest risk, clearest boundary), then fulfillment (pure downstream consumer), then carve the commerce core out last. team reorganizes into three pods plus a two-person platform group building shared infrastructure (event plumbing, deploy tooling, observability). primary risks: short dual-write windows during each extraction where a bug could cause data drift (mitigated by automated reconciliation and feature-flagged cutovers); discipline on event contracts between services (mitigated by a schema registry and contract tests from day one); customer-visible consistency gaps such as briefly stale stock counts after a purchase (mitigated by targeted UX messaging and sub-second read paths on the critical flows). end state: sub-three-minute test suites per service, independent deploys, no merge-train coupling, and eighteen months of runway remaining to compound the gains — shipping faster, onboarding faster, and scaling the team without re-introducing the coupling we are paying to remove.

@flint v0 hybrid
G: day1_split_health_signals
C: five_slos ∧ alert_thresholds ∧ canary_vs_control ∧ dashboard_groupings ∧ no_distributed_tx ∧ strangler_cutover_active
P: slo1_order_to_paid_latency_p99_under_"5s" ∧ slo2_reserve_success_rate_above_"99.5pct" ∧ slo3_saga_compensation_rate_under_"1pct" ∧ slo4_event_consumer_lag_p99_under_"5s" ∧ slo5_order_history_read_your_writes_under_"500ms" ∧ alert_page_on_two_window_breach_of_"5min" ∧ alert_warn_on_single_window_breach ∧ canary_shadow_"5pct_traffic" ∧ control_is_monolith_path ∧ compare_same_user_cohort_same_hour ∧ dashboard_grouped_by_service_then_saga_state_then_slo_tier
V: order_to_paid_p99_measured_on_saga_step_log_timestamps ∧ reserve_success_from_inventory_grpc_status ∧ compensation_rate_equals_cancelling_plus_refunding_over_confirmed ∧ consumer_lag_from_kafka_committed_offset_minus_head ∧ ryw_from_synthetic_probe_post_then_get ∧ canary_delta_significant_at_"p<0.01" ∧ dashboards_load_under_"2s" ∧ every_slo_has_runbook_link ∧ alert_routes_to_owning_pod_pagerduty
A: define_five_slos_in_prometheus_recording_rules ∧ set_burn_rate_alerts_"2pct_budget_1h_and_5pct_6h" ∧ wire_canary_via_feature_flag_"split_commerce_v1" ∧ stratify_comparison_by_user_id_hash_mod_"20" ∧ build_grafana_folder_"orders_split_day1" ∧ panels_per_service_commerce_core_inventory_fulfillment ∧ panels_per_saga_state_showing_dwell_time_and_exit_rate ∧ add_synthetic_canary_job_every_"30s" ∧ page_oncall_on_slo1_or_slo2_breach ∧ warn_only_on_slo3_slo4_slo5 ∧ freeze_rollout_if_canary_worse_than_control_by_"10pct"

@flint v0 hybrid
G: audit_jwt_module
C: python_pyjwt ∧ hs256_rs256_none_allowed ∧ hardcoded_secret ∧ exp_verification_disabled
P: alg_none_bypass_critical ∧ algorithm_confusion_hs_rs_critical ∧ hardcoded_weak_secret_critical ∧ exp_not_verified_high ∧ no_iss_aud_nbf_validation_medium ∧ missing_kid_rotation_medium ∧ verify_trusts_header_alg_high ∧ no_signature_check_on_get_unverified_header_info ∧ role_claim_unsigned_trust_high ∧ permissive_role_default_user_low
V: remove_none_from_algos ∧ split_hs_and_rs_keys ∧ pin_single_algorithm_per_key ∧ load_secret_from_env ∧ rotate_leaked_secret ∧ enable_verify_exp ∧ validate_iss_aud_nbf ∧ enforce_kid_lookup ∧ unit_tests_reject_alg_none_and_confusion
A: rank("1_critical_alg_none") ∧ rank("2_critical_algorithm_confusion_hs_rs") ∧ rank("3_critical_hardcoded_secret_in_source") ∧ rank("4_high_verify_exp_false_disables_expiry") ∧ rank("5_high_algo_taken_from_untrusted_header") ∧ rank("6_medium_no_iss_aud_nbf_checks") ∧ rank("7_medium_no_kid_or_key_rotation") ∧ rank("8_low_default_role_fallback_to_user")

1. alg=none bypass — most exploitable, zero crypto needed.
Attacker forges token, sets header alg to "none", omits signature.
jwt.decode called with algorithms=['HS256','RS256','none'] accepts unsigned token.
Payload:
header: {"alg":"none","typ":"JWT"}
body: {"sub":"victim","role":"admin","exp":9999999999}
sig: empty
Wire form: base64url(header) + "." + base64url(body) + "."
Result: require_admin passes, full admin takeover.

2. HS/RS algorithm confusion — exploitable if RS256 public key ever reaches this code as SECRET, or if attacker can coerce verify to treat public key as HMAC secret.
Since SECRET here is shared HMAC key, classic confusion variant: attacker who leaks or guesses public key from any RS256 deployment signs HS256 token using that public key bytes as HMAC secret. Current code accepts both HS256 and RS256 against same SECRET constant, so any token signed with HS256(SECRET) is trusted regardless of intended algo.
Payload:
header: {"alg":"HS256"}
body: {"sub":"victim","role":"admin","exp":9999999999}
sig: HMAC-SHA256(public_key_pem_bytes, header+"."+body)
Result: forged admin token validated as RS256-equivalent trust.

3. Expired token replay — verify_exp disabled, so any leaked token works forever.
Attacker steals token once (log, XSS, proxy, backup) and reuses past stated exp.
Payload: captured token verbatim, even years old.
body claim exp ignored by decode, role claim preserved.
Result: permanent session hijack, no refresh needed, no revocation possible without key rotation.

Shared root cause: algorithms list includes none, single SECRET used across asymmetric and symmetric algos, verify_exp=False. Fixing decode call fixes all three.

@flint v0 hybrid
G: write_pytest_regression_suite_for_fixed_jwt_service
C: pytest ∧ pyjwt ∧ fixed_verify_signature ∧ secret_from_env ∧ hs256_only
P: cover_alg_none_rejection ∧ cover_mismatched_algo_rejection ∧ cover_expired_token_rejection ∧ cover_forged_signature_rejection ∧ cover_role_spoof_via_payload_manipulation
V: all_five_attacks_raise ∧ valid_token_passes ∧ admin_role_gate_enforced ∧ tests_isolated_via_monkeypatch_env
A: code("
import base64, json, time, hmac, hashlib
import jwt as pyjwt
import pytest

@pytest.fixture(autouse=True)
def _env(monkeypatch):
    monkeypatch.setenv('JWT_SECRET', 'unit-test-secret')
    monkeypatch.setenv('JWT_CUTOVER_TS', str(int(time.time())))
    import importlib, auth.jwt_service as m
    importlib.reload(m)
    return m

def _b64(obj):
    raw = json.dumps(obj, separators=(',', ':')).encode()
    return base64.urlsafe_b64encode(raw).rstrip(b'=').decode()

def test_valid_token_roundtrip(_env):
    tok = _env.sign('alice', 'user')
    out = _env.verify(tok)
    assert out.sub == 'alice' and out.role == 'user'

def test_alg_none_rejected(_env):
    header = _b64({'alg': 'none', 'typ': 'JWT'})
    body = _b64({'sub': 'mallory', 'role': 'admin', 'exp': int(time.time()) + 3600})
    forged = f'{header}.{body}.'
    with pytest.raises(Exception):
        _env.verify(forged)

def test_mismatched_algo_rejected(_env):
    tok = pyjwt.encode(
        {'sub': 'mallory', 'role': 'admin', 'exp': int(time.time()) + 3600},
        'unit-test-secret',
        algorithm='HS512',
    )
    with pytest.raises(Exception):
        _env.verify(tok)

def test_expired_token_rejected(_env):
    tok = pyjwt.encode(
        {'sub': 'alice', 'role': 'user', 'iat': int(time.time()) - 7200, 'exp': int(time.time()) - 3600},
        'unit-test-secret',
        algorithm='HS256',
    )
    with pytest.raises(Exception):
        _env.verify(tok)

def test_forged_signature_rejected(_env):
    good = _env.sign('alice', 'user')
    head, body, sig = good.split('.')
    tampered = f'{head}.{body}.{sig[:-4]}AAAA'
    with pytest.raises(Exception):
        _env.verify(tampered)

def test_role_spoof_via_payload_manipulation_rejected(_env):
    good = _env.sign('alice', 'user')
    head, body, sig = good.split('.')
    decoded = json.loads(base64.urlsafe_b64decode(body + '==').decode())
    decoded['role'] = 'admin'
    new_body = _b64(decoded)
    spoofed = f'{head}.{new_body}.{sig}'
    with pytest.raises(Exception):
        _env.verify(spoofed)

def test_require_admin_rejects_user(_env):
    tok = _env.sign('alice', 'user')
    with pytest.raises(PermissionError):
        _env.require_admin(tok)

def test_require_admin_accepts_admin(_env):
    tok = _env.sign('root', 'admin')
    assert _env.require_admin(tok).role == 'admin'
")

Security review of our authentication module uncovered three serious defects in how we validate login tokens. Tokens can be forged without any cryptographic knowledge because our verifier accepts tokens that declare themselves unsigned. A second flaw allows an attacker who obtains any related cryptographic key material to mint tokens that impersonate administrators. A third flaw means expired tokens continue to work indefinitely, so any session token that leaks, through a support log, a stolen laptop, or a browser extension, grants permanent access until we rotate keys. In combination, these defects permit full administrative takeover of customer accounts with no user interaction.

Business risk is material. An attacker exploiting these flaws could read or modify any customer record, escalate to admin within tenant environments, and act on behalf of any user without detection in current logs. Because expired tokens remain valid, we must assume historical exposure: any token ever logged, cached, or captured upstream could still be active today. Reputational impact of a public incident would be high given expectations of enterprise-grade auth, and regulated customers may treat this as a breach of contractual security commitments.

Remediation is ready and low risk. Engineering has a tested patch that pins token validation to strong settings, enforces expiry, and reads signing material from secure configuration rather than source code. Patch preserves compatibility with tokens already issued through a 30-day grandfather window, so customers will not be logged out. We recommend expedited deploy within 48 hours, paired with immediate rotation of signing keys and invalidation of long-lived sessions. Cost of delay rises daily because any copy of our repository history contains exploitable material.

On disclosure, we have no confirmed evidence of exploitation, but several obligations likely apply and warrant legal review now rather than after deploy. Contracts with enterprise customers commonly require notification of material security defects regardless of confirmed breach. Depending on data residency, GDPR Article 33 and equivalent US state statutes may require regulator notification within 72 hours if we later find evidence of unauthorized access. Recommend Legal, Security, and Customer Success align today on notification posture, draft customer communication in parallel with deploy, and preserve logs for forensic review before key rotation erases diagnostic signal.

@flint v0 hybrid
G: diagnose_checkout_outage_pattern
C: pool_size_20 ∧ active_20_waiting_47_to_61 ∧ acquire_timeout_5000ms ∧ reaper_scans_8m_rows ∧ reaper_runs_every_23min ∧ reaper_reaps_140k_sessions_in_4s
P: reaper_holds_long_txn_on_sessions_table ∧ reaper_starves_pool ∧ checkout_cannot_acquire_connection ∧ rollback_exception_from_timeout ∧ pattern_repeats_at_"22:04" ∧ pattern_repeats_at_"22:27" ∧ pattern_repeats_at_"22:50" ∧ cadence_approx_23min
V: recovery_at_"22:04:22"_matches_reaper_end ∧ outage_window_equals_reaper_duration ∧ pool_waiting_drops_to_0_post_reap ∧ three_identical_cycles_observed
A: root_cause_is_scheduled_reaper_contention ∧ fix_batch_reaper_in_small_chunks ∧ fix_use_separate_pool_or_readonly_replica ∧ fix_add_index_on_expired_sessions ∧ fix_stagger_off_peak ∧ fix_raise_pool_size_as_mitigation_only

Reaper holds long-running transaction on sessions table while scanning 8M rows.

Reaper likely grabs connection from same pool checkout uses, or takes row/table locks checkout needs.

Pool size 20, reaper consumes 1+ connections for 4+ seconds while doing bulk DELETE.

Bulk delete of 140k rows acquires many row locks, possibly escalates to page/table locks on sessions.

Checkout handler reads/writes sessions per request, blocks waiting for locks reaper holds.

Connections pile up waiting on locks, pool exhausts, new requests timeout at 5000ms.

RollbackException fires because outer transaction never started — acquire failed before begin.

Recovery at 22:04:22 aligns exactly with reaper finish at 22:04:22.330 — causal smoking gun.

Cadence ~23min (22:04, 22:27, 22:50) matches scheduler interval, not traffic pattern.

Core issue: reaper and hot path share pool + share table, with no backpressure or chunking.

Full table scan on 8M rows suggests missing index on expiry timestamp, making reaper slow and lock-heavy.

@flint v0 hybrid
G: prevent_reaper_starving_checkout_pool
C: reaper_must_keep_running ∧ session_growth_real ∧ cannot_delay_reaper ∧ shared_pool_and_table_contention ∧ full_scan_on_"8M_rows"
P: isolate_reaper_from_checkout_pool ∧ chunk_deletes_to_release_locks ∧ add_index_on_expiry_column ∧ cap_reaper_concurrency_to_one_connection
V: checkout_p99_stays_under_"500ms"_during_reap ∧ pool_waiting_stays_near_zero ∧ reaper_still_clears_backlog_within_schedule ∧ no_lock_wait_on_sessions_during_reap
A: create_index("CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_sessions_expires_at ON sessions(expires_at) WHERE expires_at IS NOT NULL") ∧ add_dedicated_pool("reaperPool: maxSize=2, separate from checkoutPool") ∧ chunked_delete("DELETE FROM sessions WHERE id IN (SELECT id FROM sessions WHERE expires_at < now() LIMIT 1000) -- loop until zero rows, sleep 50ms between batches") ∧ reaper_uses_reaper_pool_only ∧ statement_timeout("SET LOCAL statement_timeout = '500ms'") ∧ lock_timeout("SET LOCAL lock_timeout = '100ms'")

On the night in question, our checkout service experienced three brief windows of elevated errors between 22:04 and 22:50. During each window, customers attempting to complete a purchase saw failures or long delays lasting roughly 20 to 40 seconds before service recovered on its own. In total, approximately 23 minutes of degraded checkout availability were observed across three recurring incidents. No customer data was lost or exposed, and no payments were processed incorrectly — requests that failed simply did not complete and could be retried.

Impact was limited to new checkout attempts during those narrow windows. Customers already past the checkout step were unaffected. Browsing, cart management, account pages, and all other services continued to operate normally throughout. For customers whose checkout attempt failed during an affected window, the transaction was never charged and the order was never created, so a simple retry would have succeeded.

Root cause traces to an interaction between two parts of our system that, individually, both behaved as designed. A scheduled cleanup job runs periodically to remove expired login sessions and keep our database healthy. That cleanup job and the checkout service happened to share the same pool of database connections and the same underlying table. When cleanup ran, it held onto enough resources for a few seconds that checkout requests could not get a database connection of their own, and they timed out. Once cleanup finished, checkout recovered immediately. Because cleanup runs on a fixed schedule, the problem repeated three times before we mitigated it.

We have already shipped changes to prevent this from recurring. Cleanup now runs on its own isolated connection pool, so it cannot starve checkout of resources. We added a database index that makes the cleanup job dramatically faster and lighter on locks. Cleanup now operates in small batches rather than one large sweep, which means it releases resources frequently instead of holding them for seconds at a time. We also added stricter timeouts so that, even in unexpected situations, cleanup cannot block user-facing traffic.

Finally, we are adding monitoring that would have alerted us to this specific pattern — checkout pool saturation correlated with scheduled background work — in under a minute rather than after customer impact. We are sorry for the disruption, and we appreciate your patience. If you experienced a failed checkout during this window and have any concerns about your order, please reach out to support and we will make it right.

Honest read: this was not a coding mistake so much as gap in how we reason about shared resources. Reaper and checkout were built by different people at different times, and each component looked correct in isolation. What we lacked was any forcing function that would have made someone ask "what else uses this connection pool, and what happens when both are busy at once?" A simple architecture review checklist that flagged any new background job touching production tables shared with user-facing paths would have surfaced this during design. We have talked about load-testing scheduled jobs under concurrent user traffic for a while; this outage moves that from nice-to-have to table stakes.

Second gap was observability of slow-building failure modes. Pool saturation is not a sudden event — waiting queue climbed from zero to 47 over hundreds of milliseconds, and we had no alert on queue depth, only on error rate and latency after customers already felt it. Had we been watching connection pool health as first-class signal alongside request metrics, on-call would have been paged before first 500 went out. More broadly, we tend to monitor what users experience rather than what predicts what users will experience, and this incident is good prompt to invert that default for our hottest paths.

Third, and most uncomfortable, staging environment does not replicate production data volume. Reaper scanning 8M rows behaves nothing like reaper scanning 50k rows, and our load tests never exercised that regime. We have been putting off investment in production-shaped staging data because it felt expensive and low-urgency; this outage is reminder that the cost of not having it is paid in customer trust, not engineering hours. Worth revisiting that tradeoff with fresh eyes, and probably worth treating "can we reproduce production-scale background jobs in a pre-prod environment" as blocking requirement for any new scheduled work touching user-critical tables.
